refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to acceptance, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution permits completion of load→travel→dump without artificial time constraints imposed by the system itself. Measure p95 cycle time against CT_target as an acceptance criterion; if p95 > CT_target, root-cause analysis determines whether the FR was not met (system prevented timely execution) or whether operational/environmental factors degraded performance despite the FR being satisfied.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, and allocate execution control to a supervisory coordinator that sequences these operations based on state transitions and sensor feedback.

DP2: Implement a parallel execution model where load, travel, and dump operations overlap where physically feasible (e.g., travel begins before load completes), with a real-time scheduler that enforces hard timing constraints and detects conflicts.

DP3: Design a fixed-sequence state machine with pre-calculated operation durations calibrated to site conditions (payload mass, terrain, distance), with adaptive timing adjustments triggered by measured cycle-time drift beyond acceptable bounds.

DP4: Establish a demand-driven pull model where each operation (load, travel, dump) is triggered by completion of the prior operation, with built-in buffers and contingency paths to handle variability in sub-operation duration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Design a fixed-sequence state machine with pre-calculated operation durations calibrated to site conditions (payload mass, terrain, distance), with adaptive timing adjustments triggered by measured cycle-time drift beyond acceptable bounds.

potential_sources: DP3 couples cycle-time performance to site-condition calibration; if calibration is incomplete or site conditions change unexpectedly, timing targets may be missed. Mitigation: explicit calibration protocol and monitoring thresholds.

DP3 assumes sub-operations (load, travel, dump) have measurable, repeatable durations; high variability in any sub-operation (e.g., load time depends on material cohesion) could violate the p95 acceptance criterion. Mitigation: characterize variability in each sub-operation and set conservative budgets.
DP3 introduces a dependency on accurate sensor feedback for state transitions; sensor failures or delays could cause timing violations. Mitigation: redundancy and timeout-based fallbacks.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is preferred over DP1 (sequential, no parallelism) because it allows adaptive correction; over DP2 (parallel) because it reduces real-time scheduling complexity and is more predictable; over DP4 (pull) because it provides deterministic timing. The fixed-sequence + adaptive adjustment pattern is well-suited to duty-cycle acceptance testing and site commissioning.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Sub-operation durations (load, travel, dump) are measurable and repeatable within acceptable variance under the agreed duty cycle. RISK: If material properties, terrain, or payload distribution vary significantly, measured cycle times may exceed p95 target. MITIGATION: Conduct pre-deployment characterization of variability; set timing budgets conservatively.

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (distance, terrain, material type) are known and stable during the acceptance test window. RISK: Seasonal changes, equipment wear, or operator technique variations could invalidate calibration. MITIGATION: Define site-condition envelope in acceptance criteria; schedule testing during representative conditions.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive timing adjustments can be applied without violating safety constraints (e.g., no unsafe acceleration or deceleration). RISK: Aggressive timing corrections could compromise operator safety or equipment integrity. MITIGATION: Bound adjustment rates and validate against safety plan.

ASSUMPTION: Supervisory coordinator and state machine logic are implemented with sufficient determinism and latency predictability. RISK: Software scheduling jitter or I/O delays could cause timing violations. MITIGATION: Use real-time OS or deterministic execution framework; validate latency budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits the cycle-time optimization activities (e.g., repeated test cycles, sensor instrumentation). RISK: Safety constraints may limit test duration or operational modes. MITIGATION: Align acceptance test protocol with safety plan early; involve safety stakeholder.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle such that the measured p95 cycle time is ≤ CT_target under the agreed duty cycle, site conditions (specified envelope: distance range, material type, payload mass range), and operational policies. Cycle-time performance shall be validated during site commissioning with a minimum of N representative test cycles (N to be agreed). Adaptive timing adjustments shall not violate the worksite safety plan or equipment operating limits.' This revision clarifies the acceptance test protocol, bounds site-condition variability, and ensures safety alignment before DP realization.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Autonomous vehicle control logic, state machine execution, timing calibration, and cycle-time monitoring with adaptive adjustment mechanisms

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle reports measured cycle-time, state transitions, timing adjustments, and operational metrics to enable drift detection and external monitoring

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives mission parameters (payload mass, terrain type, distance) to calibrate pre-calculated operation durations at mission start

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle signals safety-critical conditions if adaptive timing adjustments exceed acceptable bounds or state machine enters unsafe states

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle may receive remote override or recalibration commands; transmits state machine status for teleoperation awareness

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: state machine execution, timing calibration, and cycle-time monitoring are core vehicle autonomy functions. Pre-calculated durations and adaptive adjustments are intrinsic to vehicle control logic. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and tasking input cross boundaries; safety alerts are properly delegated to Safety Services via interface.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not an autonomous control executor. State machine logic, calibration, and adaptive timing belong in the vehicle autonomy layer. High diffusion: core state machine would leak into Vehicle anyway; teleoperation would only observe and override, not own execution.

Poor fit: Safety Services monitors and enforces safety constraints, not executes operational state machines. Cycle-time drift detection and adaptive timing are operational concerns, not safety enforcement. High diffusion: state machine logic must reside in Vehicle; Safety Services would only validate bounds and trigger alerts.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP3 here violates separation of concerns and creates a monolithic, undecomposable boundary. Extreme diffusion: all vehicle, teleoperation, and safety logic would be entangled.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module owns the fixed-sequence state machine, pre-calculated operation durations, and adaptive timing adjustment logic because these are intrinsic to autonomous vehicle control. Tasking provides mission parameters (payload, terrain, distance) to calibrate durations at mission start. Telemetry exports measured cycle-time and adjustment metrics. SafetyAlert notifies Safety Services when adaptive adjustments exceed acceptable bounds. Network Link enables remote recalibration if needed. This containment preserves cohesion (all state machine logic in one module) and minimizes coupling (only four well-defined interfaces cross the boundary).
containment_risks: Risk: If cycle-time drift detection logic is implemented as a separate monitoring service outside Vehicle, adaptive adjustment decisions may be delayed or decoupled from state machine execution, reducing responsiveness.
Risk: If pre-calculated duration calibration is delegated to Tasking or an external service, Vehicle loses autonomy to adjust timing in real-time based on measured drift, forcing re-tasking cycles.
Risk: If Safety Services is tightly coupled to state machine internals to enforce timing bounds, Vehicle and Safety Services become interdependent, violating separation of concerns.

Risk: If Network Link enables frequent remote recalibration of durations, Vehicle state machine may become unstable due to mid-mission parameter changes; recommend recalibration only at mission boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: STRONG. Acceptance criterion 'Measured cycle time p95 ≤ CT_target' is directly verifiable via Telemetry interface (Vehicle exports measured cycle-time and state transitions). Adaptive timing adjustments are observable and loggable. Site conditions (payload mass, terrain, distance) are captured in Tasking input and used to calibrate pre-calculated durations. Worksite safety plan compliance is enforced via SafetyAlert interface delegation to Safety Services. All verification touchpoints are architecturally clear.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. Vehicle module owns the complete FR realization: (1) state machine execution ensures load→travel→dump sequencing; (2) pre-calculated durations calibrated to site conditions via Tasking input; (3) adaptive timing adjustments triggered by measured cycle-time drift; (4) cycle-time monitoring via Telemetry export. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility. Interfaces are well-defined and minimal (4 external boundaries). Safety delegation to Safety Services via SafetyAlert is clean separation of concerns.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE RISK: Cycle-time drift detection logic must remain tightly coupled to state machine execution within Vehicle module. If implemented as a separate external monitoring service, adaptive adjustment decisions will be delayed, reducing responsiveness to real-time drift. Recommend drift detection as an internal Vehicle function, not a separate module.

MODERATE RISK: Pre-calculated duration calibration must be owned by Vehicle at mission start (via Tasking input). If delegated to external service or Teleoperation layer, Vehicle loses autonomy to adjust timing in real-time based on measured drift. Recommend calibration logic remain internal to Vehicle.

LOW RISK: Network Link enables remote recalibration commands. If recalibration occurs mid-mission, state machine may become unstable due to parameter changes during active cycle execution. Recommend policy: recalibration only at mission boundaries or via explicit state machine reset.

LOW RISK: SafetyAlert delegation to Safety Services is correct, but Safety Services must NOT be tightly coupled to state machine internals to enforce timing bounds. Recommend Safety Services validates bounds asynchronously and triggers alerts; Vehicle remains autonomous in execution logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT. Vehicle module scores 0.92 containment / 0.08 diffusion. State machine execution, timing calibration, and cycle-time monitoring are intrinsic to autonomous vehicle control and properly localized. Telemetry export and Tasking input are minimal, well-defined boundary crossings. Alternative candidates (Teleoperation, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) all score poorly (0.15–0.65 containment) due to misalignment with operational autonomy ownership. Vehicle is the correct and only viable IM for DP3. No diffusion of core state machine logic across multiple modules detected.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 (fixed-sequence state machine with pre-calculated durations and adaptive timing adjustments) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (enable load→travel→dump cycle within customer-defined cycle time target). State machine ensures correct sequencing; pre-calculated durations calibrated to site conditions (payload, terrain, distance) provide baseline timing; adaptive adjustments triggered by measured drift enable real-time convergence to CT_target. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' is achievable via this design approach. No logical gaps or unaddressed functional requirements detected.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (Drift Detection Coupling): Explicitly document that cycle-time drift detection is an internal Vehicle function, not delegated to external monitoring service. Specify drift threshold (e.g., ±5% of CT_target) and adjustment trigger logic within state machine. Ensure Telemetry export includes drift magnitude and adjustment magnitude for external observability.

REVISION 2 (Calibration Autonomy): Clarify that pre-calculated duration calibration occurs at mission start (via Tasking input) and is owned by Vehicle. Document that Vehicle may perform in-mission micro-adjustments (within ±X% bounds) based on measured drift, but major recalibration requires mission reset. Recommend policy: Network Link recalibration commands are queued for next mission boundary, not applied mid-cycle.

REVISION 3 (Safety Boundary Clarity): Specify that SafetyAlert triggers when adaptive adjustments exceed acceptable bounds (e.g., cumulative adjustment > ±10% of baseline duration). Safety Services validates these bounds and enforces worksite safety policy, but does NOT own state machine logic. Document that Vehicle remains autonomous in execution; Safety Services is a constraint validator, not a co-executor.

REVISION 4 (Acceptance Criterion Mapping): Explicitly link acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target' to Telemetry export. Specify that Vehicle logs all cycle-time measurements, state transitions, and adaptive adjustments. Recommend post-mission analysis: compute p95 from logged data under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. This closes the verification loop from DP to acceptance criterion.

REVISION 5 (Containment Boundary Finalization): Confirm Vehicle module scope includes: (a) state machine definition and execution, (b) pre-calculated duration tables indexed by (payload, terrain, distance), (c) drift detection and adaptive adjustment logic, (d) cycle-time measurement and logging. Exclude: teleoperation UI (Teleoperation module), safety policy enforcement (Safety Services module), mission tasking (external Tasking interface). This prevents scope creep and maintains cohesion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with strong technical merit. However, the recommended_revisions expose critical specification gaps that must be closed BEFORE proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The safety criticality factor (high) amplifies the risk of proceeding with ambiguous boundaries around drift detection coupling, calibration autonomy, and safety constraint validation. Revisions 1–5 are not cosmetic; they establish the precise ownership and behavioral contracts that sub-level FRs will depend upon. Specifically: (1) drift detection logic scope must be unambiguous to avoid diffusion when decomposing cycle-time monitoring; (2) calibration autonomy boundaries must be explicit to prevent sub-FRs from inadvertently re-delegating to external services; (3) safety boundary clarity is essential for high-safety-criticality systems to ensure sub-FRs do not create unsafe coupling between Vehicle autonomy and Safety Services enforcement. Revising now prevents rework at lower levels and ensures sub-FRs inherit a well-defined, unambiguous parent specification.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound and passes the gate, but its specification lacks the precision required for safe, unambiguous sub-FR derivation in a high-safety-criticality system. Specifically: (1) 'adaptive timing adjustments' must explicitly define drift detection as an internal Vehicle function with threshold and trigger logic; (2) 'pre-calculated durations calibrated to site conditions' must clarify mission-start ownership and in-mission adjustment bounds; (3) interaction with SafetyAlert must specify that Safety Services validates bounds asynchronously without owning state machine logic. Revise DP3 to incorporate Revisions 1–3 and 5 from recommended_revisions. This produces a tighter, more implementable DP specification that will support unambiguous sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Drift detection threshold (e.g., ±5% of CT_target) is acceptable to stakeholders and achievable within Vehicle module computational budget.
Pre-calculated duration tables (indexed by payload, terrain, distance) can be generated offline and loaded at mission start without unacceptable latency.
In-mission micro-adjustments (within ±X% bounds) do not violate worksite safety constraints or customer cycle-time SLA.
SafetyAlert interface supports asynchronous constraint validation without blocking Vehicle state machine execution.
Network Link recalibration commands can be queued and deferred to mission boundaries without customer dissatisfaction.
Telemetry export bandwidth and logging capacity are sufficient to capture all cycle-time measurements, state transitions, and adjustment magnitudes for post-mission analysis.
open_risks: SAFETY RISK (High): If drift detection logic is not tightly coupled to state machine execution, adaptive adjustments may lag real-time drift, causing cycle-time to exceed CT_target and violate safety margins. Mitigation: Enforce drift detection as internal Vehicle function; specify maximum detection latency (e.g., <100ms).

SAFETY RISK (High): If SafetyAlert constraint validation is synchronous and blocks state machine, Vehicle may become unresponsive during safety checks. If asynchronous, Safety Services may fail to prevent unsafe adjustments. Mitigation: Clarify SafetyAlert contract (sync vs. async); specify timeout and fallback behavior.

OPERATIONAL RISK (Moderate): If in-mission micro-adjustment bounds (±X%) are too loose, cumulative drift may exceed acceptable limits. If too tight, Vehicle may be unable to converge to CT_target. Mitigation: Conduct sensitivity analysis on adjustment bounds; validate against duty cycle and site condition ranges.

ARCHITECTURAL RISK (Moderate): If pre-calculated duration calibration is not owned exclusively by Vehicle at mission start, multiple modules may attempt to adjust timing, causing state machine instability. Mitigation: Enforce single-owner pattern; document that only Vehicle owns calibration logic.

VERIFICATION RISK (Moderate): If Telemetry export does not include drift magnitude and adjustment magnitude, post-mission analysis cannot validate that adaptive adjustments are functioning correctly. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry schema to include all adjustment events with timestamps and magnitudes.
evidence_needed: Revised DP3 specification incorporating Revisions 1–5, with explicit drift detection threshold, calibration autonomy policy, and safety boundary clarity.
SafetyAlert interface contract: specify whether constraint validation is synchronous or asynchronous, timeout behavior, and fallback logic if Safety Services is unavailable.

Telemetry schema definition: confirm that Vehicle exports cycle-time measurements, state transitions, drift magnitude, and adjustment magnitude with sufficient granularity for post-mission p95 analysis.
Pre-calculated duration table specification: define indexing scheme (payload, terrain, distance), table generation process, and loading mechanism at mission start.
In-mission adjustment bounds policy: specify ±X% bounds, cumulative adjustment limits, and conditions under which Vehicle triggers SafetyAlert.
Duty cycle and site condition ranges: confirm that adaptive adjustment logic can converge to CT_target across all expected operational scenarios (payload range, terrain types, distance ranges).
Safety analysis: confirm that drift detection latency, adjustment bounds, and SafetyAlert interaction do not create unsafe coupling or race conditions.
